teaching: I am not enough is one of the most common core wounds, and it often takes root in childhood through critical parents, comparison with older siblings, or having caregivers who put emphasis on your achievements but withheld warmth when you didn't perform. Sometimes a single painful event — feeling rejected by a friend, not being picked, hearing 'you should be more like your sister' — gets stored and reprojected outward as a permanent feature of who you are. The wound shows up later as insecurity, perfectionism, hypersensitivity to rejection, and a habit of mulling over interactions afterward, asking 'did I do enough? Was I enough?' What's really happening is your nervous system running a filter that scans every interaction for evidence of your insufficiency — and finding it, because that's what it was trained to look for. To reprogram this, the practice isn't about finding monumentally impressive evidence. It's about consistently showing your nervous system small, true moments where you were enough. A hack that helps: start general and get specific. If 'I am enough' feels too big, start with 'I am becoming more capable each day' and build momentum from there. Repetition and emotion matter more than the size of the evidence. Each small acknowledgment retrains your reticular activating system to start scanning for sufficiency instead of insufficiency, and over weeks of practice, your default lens shifts.
gentlePrompts: Think of someone you consider 'on your level' — someone you'd call good enough. If they are, you are.
Think of something small you handled this week that you weren't asked to do. That was enough.

teaching: I am abandoned typically forms from inconsistency in childhood — a caregiver who was loving but worked a lot, attunement that was withdrawn suddenly, perceived abandonment like being left at school and forgotten, or emotional disconnection during important moments. The fear of abandonment is one of only three biological fears we're born with, which is why this wound runs so deep when it's triggered. As an adult, it shows up as anxiety in relationships, mulling over silences and disconnects, clinging or overcompensating to earn closeness, staying in unhealthy relationships far too long because the wound of leaving feels worse than staying. You might find yourself contorting into whoever you think people want you to be in order to avoid this feeling being activated. The painful irony is that the more you abandon yourself trying to avoid being abandoned by others, the more the wound gets reinforced. Reprogramming this requires two parallel moves. First — look for togetherness and connection in your existing life. Career, friends, family, anyone where you feel chosen and stayed-with. Second, and more importantly — stop abandoning yourself. Notice when you're suppressing a feeling, ignoring a need, or violating your own boundary to keep someone close. Coming back to yourself is the deepest antidote, because it breaks the cycle at its root. Repetition and emotion will rewire your subconscious to expect being cherished instead of left.
gentlePrompts: Think of someone whose face changes when they see you.
Think of someone who came back, even after a hard moment.

aiPhrasing: times you have not been alone
teaching: I am alone often goes hand in hand with abandonment but has its own distinct flavor — it's the felt sense of facing things by yourself, of nobody really being there in the way you need. It often originates from feeling emotionally neglected even when people were physically present — caregivers who were busy, distracted, or dealing with their own pain. You may have grown up in a full house and still felt deeply alone. The emotions that come with this wound are sadness, fear, helplessness, and sometimes a numbness that protects you from feeling the depth of it. Behaviorally, this can lead you to either cling tightly to relationships to never feel alone, or paradoxically withdraw because reaching out feels too risky. People with this wound often abandon themselves emotionally — disconnecting from their own feelings, needs, and inner experience, which actually deepens the aloneness. The reprogramming work has two layers. First, look for togetherness in the relationships you already have — moments where someone's presence made the room feel different, where you weren't facing something alone. Second, build connection to yourself first. Journal, check in with your body, attend to your own emotions like someone you love would. The more present you become to yourself, the less the world can make you feel alone — because you become reliable company.
gentlePrompts: Think of who you'd actually call if something genuinely went wrong tonight.
Think of a moment when someone's presence made the room feel different.

teaching: I am unloved often pairs with unworthy and tends to come from family distress in childhood — divorce, a parent's grief, emotional neglect, abuse, or a parent who only gave love when you performed. The deepest version of this wound is the inference that love has to be earned through what you do, not given for who you are. Conscious parenting research talks about the importance of going to a child for no reason — just to give love when nothing has been earned — and the absence of that creates the belief that love is conditional. As an adult, this wound shows up as overcompensating in relationships, giving to earn love, resentment when the giving isn't returned in kind, and a fear of receiving because being given love unconditionally feels foreign and unsafe. People with this wound block intimacy without realizing it, push people away when they get too close, and often settle for relationships where they have to work hard for affection. The reprogramming hack here is critical: belief work alone won't shift this. The deepest healing comes from behaviorally giving yourself love unconditionally — kindness when you mess up, care when you're tired, encouragement when you're struggling. Re-parent yourself. Be the parent who would have given love for no reason. That breaks the cycle at the level of the wound, and over time your subconscious learns that you are loved — because you are loving yourself.
gentlePrompts: Think of someone whose face changes when they see you.
Think of love that's expressed without words — through showing up, remembering, asking how you are.

teaching: I am bad typically forms when parents shame the child for mistakes instead of naming the action — saying 'you are bad' instead of 'what you did wasn't good.' Repeated enough with emotion, this gets hardwired as a core identity. It can also come from authoritarian parents who rule through guilt, religious or cultural environments that emphasize being good to be worthy of love, or growing up parentified — caretaking for a struggling parent and feeling that your mistakes might push them over the edge. The wound shows up as shame, guilt, perfectionism, lying to avoid humiliation, over-apologizing, taking on excessive responsibility for other people's feelings, and self-destructive behavior in extreme cases — because if you're bad, you don't deserve good things. Here's the critical reframe: when you're reprogramming this wound, do not look for why you are good. Look for why you are innocent. The good/bad binary is part of what created the wound in the first place. Goodness implies you have to perform; innocence acknowledges your humanity. Look for what programs were running when you made a mistake, what stresses you were carrying, what you were doing your best with. You can be accountable for an action without making yourself bad as a person. Reprogramming this is about humanizing yourself — ending the punishment-reward cycle that traumatized you, and giving yourself the empathy, compassion, and grace that you weren't given.
gentlePrompts: Think of a mistake you made recently — what was happening in your life at the time? What programs were running? You were doing your best with what you had.
Think of how you'd describe someone else doing the exact thing you did. Would you call them bad — or human?

teaching: I am weak often forms from being exposed to circumstances beyond what you could handle as a child — physical abuse between parents, bullying, having needs but no power to get them met, or seeing a parent who you empathized with be weak in their own relationship. When we want to do something but can't, the mind makes the emotional inference 'I am too small, too weak, too defenseless' and stores it as identity. The emotions that come with this wound are helplessness, despair, frustration, sometimes a why-bother mentality, and a kind of frozen state where you feel like there's no point trying. It can lead to learned helplessness — staying stuck in situations you don't want to be in, avoiding opportunities, putting yourself down before anyone else can. Reprogramming this wound requires recognizing that the things you've endured already are proof of strength. People with this wound have often put up with circumstances that would have broken others, while not crediting themselves for the resilience that took. Look across all seven areas of life for evidence of strength — character traits, hard things you got through, boundaries you held, moments of courage. The reprogramming hack is to take one strong or courageous action every single day, however small, and consciously credit yourself for it. Speaking up, setting a small boundary, choosing growth over comfort. Repetition plus emotion will retrain your nervous system to recognize the strength that has been there all along.
gentlePrompts: Think of a hard thing you got through that you didn't think you could.
Think of a moment when you stayed standing when something tried to knock you down.

teaching: I am unsafe often comes from scary events in childhood — accidents, witnessing parental conflict, instability in the home, having a caregiver who modeled anxiety or worst-case thinking, or just not receiving enough secure attunement in the early years when your nervous system was forming. The wound shows up as chronic anxiety, hypervigilance, worst-case scenario thinking, an obsession with control, and difficulty trusting that the future will be okay. You might find yourself waiting for the other shoe to drop, terrorizing yourself with what-if scenarios, or staying in comfort zones because anything new feels dangerous. The thought patterns that come with this wound are subtle but pervasive — 'something bad is going to happen,' 'I can't trust this,' 'I have to be prepared for everything.' Of all the wounds, this one is most powerfully reprogrammed through the nervous system. Your body has to learn safety, not just your mind. Equilibration matters most here — relaxing your shoulders, opening your chest, taking deep breaths, grounding your feet on the floor. These actions tell your subconscious you are safe right now, in this moment. The belief work matters too: look across your life for evidence of where you are safe right now. Roof over your head, people who care about you, your own capacity to handle what comes. The trick is anchoring into the present, because most of this wound's pain lives in projected futures that haven't happened.
gentlePrompts: Think of a place where your shoulders drop the moment you walk in.
Think of a person whose presence settles your body.

aiPhrasing: times your mind has worked well
teaching: I am stupid often forms from being shamed for not understanding something, comparison with siblings or peers, struggling in a school environment that didn't fit your intelligence style, or having parents who valued certain kinds of smart and dismissed others. Sometimes it's a single painful moment — being laughed at for an answer, being told you're slow — that gets stored as identity. The wound shows up as self-deprecation, fear of speaking up in meetings or groups, avoiding learning opportunities because failing would confirm the belief, and an internal voice that critiques your thinking before you've even finished a thought. Intelligence comes in many forms — analytical, emotional, creative, social, mechanical, intuitive — and this wound often forms because someone evaluated you on a narrow definition and you took on their measure as truth. The reprogramming work is to recognize the kinds of intelligence you actually have, not the kinds you were judged for not having. Look across the seven areas: where do you read people well, solve problems creatively, understand systems, learn from experience? You might be highly intelligent in ways your childhood environment didn't recognize or value. Over time, by collecting evidence of your mind working well, you retrain your default lens to see intelligence where it exists rather than scanning for moments of confusion as proof of stupidity.
gentlePrompts: Think of a problem you figured out that others couldn't.
Think of a moment when someone said 'that's a good point' and meant it.

teaching: I am foolish is a close cousin of 'I am stupid' but has a different texture — it's not about intelligence in the analytical sense, it's about judgment. The fear of looking like a fool, of being naive, of trusting the wrong thing or person. It often forms from being mocked, humiliated, or having your earnestness exploited in childhood. Sometimes it comes from a specific moment of trusting someone who betrayed that trust, and making the inference that your judgment can't be trusted. The wound shows up as overthinking decisions, hesitating before sharing opinions, second-guessing your instincts even when they've served you well, or alternately becoming cynical to never be caught looking naive again. There's often a layer of pride wrapped around this wound — a fear of being publicly wrong that keeps you from taking risks or being genuinely open. Reprogramming this means honoring your wisdom across the seven areas of life. Wisdom isn't the absence of mistakes — it's pattern recognition, intuition trained by experience, learning from what happened. Every time you trusted your gut and were right, every time you spotted something others missed, every time you advised a friend in a way that helped them — that's wisdom. Collecting this evidence retrains your nervous system to expect that your judgment can be trusted, which lets you re-engage with life from a wiser place rather than a defensive one.
gentlePrompts: Think of a time you trusted your gut and were right.
Think of advice you gave someone that helped them.

aiPhrasing: times you have been worthy of what you received
teaching: I am unworthy often pairs with unloved and tends to come from family distress, emotional neglect, shaming, or the message that you have to earn your worth through achievement. The deepest version of this wound is the inference that you are not deserving of receiving — love, attention, success, ease, joy. It can be amplified by societies and cultures that pressure people to earn worth through productivity, appearance, or status. As an adult, this wound shows up as blocking receiving, self-sabotaging when good things come, fearing emotional closeness, going to great lengths to earn what you actually deserve to be given, and accompanying shame that makes you want to hide. You might overcompensate in relationships, give without limit, and feel resentful when it isn't reciprocated — without realizing that the underlying belief is making you treat your own worth as conditional. The reprogramming hack mirrors the unloved wound — belief work helps, but behavior is the deepest lever. Give yourself what you would have wanted to be given freely. Receive without earning. Let yourself be cared for. Practice receiving compliments without deflecting. Practice taking up space without apologizing. Over time, your nervous system learns that you are worthy because you are treating yourself as worthy, and the external world starts to mirror this back to you with surprising consistency.
gentlePrompts: Think of something you have that you earned through real effort.
Think of someone who gave you their attention or care without conditions.

teaching: I am helpless often forms in childhood from being controlled — strict or authoritarian parents, dynamics where you had no voice, no negotiation, no agency over your own choices. It can also come from witnessing painful situations you wished you could change but couldn't, like having an alcoholic parent or seeing instability in the home. The emotions that come with this wound are despair, frustration, defeat, and sometimes a frozen quality where you feel like there's no point even trying. As an adult, this can show up as learned helplessness — staying in jobs or relationships that don't serve you, not asking for what you need, or alternately overcompensating by trying to control everything around you as a reaction against ever feeling powerless again. People with this wound often forget that they have far more agency than they're using. The reprogramming work involves recognizing the empowerment you already have across the seven areas of life — choices you make daily, control you exercise over your own time, decisions that are fully yours. Then deliberately taking one empowered action per day — setting a small boundary, making a decision you've been deferring, advocating for what you want. Each action retrains your nervous system to expect agency, which compounds over weeks into a fundamentally different sense of self. The opposite of helplessness isn't being in control of everything — it's the felt knowledge that you can act, choose, and shape what's possible.
gentlePrompts: Think of something you handled on your own that surprised you.
Think of a moment when you made a choice that changed an outcome.

teaching: I am unseen often comes from having avoidant caregivers, many siblings competing for attention, parents going through their own difficulties, or strict environments where your needs and feelings weren't given space. Research on child development shows that simple eye contact in the early years has profound effects on emotional regulation — and the absence of being noticed in this fundamental way creates a deep emotional imprint. The wound shows up as a belief that there's no point speaking up because nobody hears it anyway, leading you to repress your feelings and needs, becoming the invisible person in your relationships. You might feel deep frustration or hurt when interrupted or dismissed, and either withdraw to meet your own needs in isolation or eventually explode in anger when the buildup becomes too much. The most important reprogramming work for this wound is internal first. You have to start seeing your own feelings and needs before others can. Develop emotional literacy — labeling what you're feeling, why, what you need. Then practice expressing those things outward, not to gain a particular response, but to recondition the belief that you deserve to take up space. Look for the people in your life who do see you, even the small moments of being noticed, and let those count. Over time the wound shifts as you become someone who sees yourself first, then teaches others how to see you by showing them clearly.
gentlePrompts: Think of someone who paused and noticed you recently.
Think of when someone remembered something small you'd said weeks ago.

teaching: I am unheard is a close sibling of 'I am unseen' but has its own specific texture — it's about your voice, your opinions, your needs not being received. It often forms from dismissive parents, authoritarian environments where your input wasn't welcomed, or family dynamics where the loudest or most distressed person got attention while you stayed quiet. The wound shows up as frustration when interrupted, fatigue from feeling like you've explained yourself a hundred times without being understood, and sometimes a habit of either over-explaining to make sure you're heard or going silent because what's the point. You might find yourself resentful in relationships where you give space for others but never feel reciprocated, or angry at people who seem oblivious to what you've said. The reprogramming work is twofold. First, hear yourself — write your thoughts down, voice them out loud, make space for your own opinions to land internally before you bring them to others. Second, practice speaking up, not to be guaranteed a particular response but to honor the belief that your voice matters. Even imperfect expression beats silence for this wound. Look for the conversations where you said something real and the other person took it in — those moments are evidence that you can be heard, and they retrain your nervous system to expect that landing rather than dismissal.
gentlePrompts: Think of a conversation where you said something real and the other person actually took it in.
Think of when someone changed their mind because of what you said.

teaching: I am unimportant often comes from a lack of affirmation in childhood — strict parents who didn't celebrate you, environments where your needs and desires were dismissed, or families where you felt invisible compared to other siblings or to crises happening in the household. The deeper inference is 'the things I care about don't matter, so I don't matter.' This wound shows up as not asking for what you need, putting yourself last on the totem pole, settling for less attention or affection than you actually want, and sometimes a baseline sense of being expendable. You might overcompensate by trying to earn importance through achievement, or alternately retreat into smallness because making yourself known feels presumptuous. People with this wound often abandon their own priorities, hobbies, and boundaries because at a subconscious level those things don't feel important enough to defend. The reprogramming work is making yourself matter through action. Speak your needs out loud. Put your hobbies on the calendar. Set the boundary. Take the space. Each act of treating yourself as important retrains your nervous system to expect importance, and the people around you start mirroring that back. Look for the places you already matter — the people whose day is different because you're in it, the spaces where your absence would be noticed. Mattering isn't something you have to earn. It's already true, and the practice is letting yourself receive it.
gentlePrompts: Think of someone whose day is different because you're in it.
Think of a place where your absence would be noticed.

aiPhrasing: times you have been whole exactly as you are
teaching: I am defective is one of the deepest wounds because it suggests there's something fundamentally wrong with you that can't be fixed. It often forms from being treated as a problem to be solved — having parents who tried to change you, being told you were too sensitive, too much, too quiet, too different. It can also come from being part of a family where someone was always trying to make you 'better' rather than accepting you as you are. The wound shows up as an internal sense of being broken, a habit of hiding parts of yourself, fearing intimacy because if someone really knew you they'd see the defect, and a relentless self-improvement drive aimed at fixing the unfixable thing. People with this wound often pour energy into changing themselves while never accepting themselves, which deepens the wound because the underlying message is that they're not okay as they are. Reprogramming this is about wholeness, not perfection. Look for the parts of you that are working — your capacity to love, your humor, your perseverance, your tenderness, your particular angle on the world. Make peace with the parts you've judged as defective and explore what they're actually serving. Often what we've labeled defective is just a trait that was misread by an environment that couldn't appreciate it. The deepest reprogramming comes from being seen and loved in your fullness by yourself first — knowing your imperfections and staying close to yourself anyway.
gentlePrompts: Think of someone who knows your imperfections and stays close anyway.
Think of a moment when you felt complete — nothing missing.

teaching: I don't belong often forms from feeling out of place in your family, your school, or your peer group growing up. Maybe you were the artist in a household of pragmatists, the sensitive one in a family of stoics, or the kid who never quite fit in with the kids around you. It can also form from being the new kid often, moving cities, or having something about you that made you feel marked as different. The wound has a particular flavor — it's not just exclusion, it's the deeper sense that you don't fit anywhere at all, that you might have been born in the wrong place or time. As an adult, this shows up as social anxiety, holding back in groups, scanning rooms for signs you're not welcome, and sometimes a romantic attachment to outsider identity that keeps you from letting yourself actually belong when belonging is offered. The reprogramming work involves looking specifically for places you already belong — spaces where your presence is just expected, no proving required, no contorting needed. Then practicing showing up as yourself in environments where you might have hidden before. The deep irony of this wound is that the hiding is often what keeps the belonging away — when you mask, people can't actually meet you, so the connection stays surface. Belonging starts with belonging to yourself, and from there it becomes possible to find your people.
gentlePrompts: Think of a place or group where your presence is just expected — no proving required.
Think of a moment you walked in and felt like you fit.

teaching: I am disliked often forms from early rejection — a friendship that ended painfully, being bullied, having a parent or sibling who openly criticized you, or a specific moment of being publicly humiliated that got stored as identity. The deeper roots of this wound are usually in self-rejection — there are parts of yourself you've judged as unlikable, and you project that judgment outward, assuming others see what you see. The wound shows up as social anxiety, mulling over interactions afterward, attributing neutral facial expressions to dislike, avoiding situations where you might be evaluated, and sometimes overcompensating to make yourself maximally likable, which is exhausting and prevents real connection. The reprogramming work starts with accepting the parts of yourself you've rejected. Make a list of traits you're embarrassed by or hide, and find the other side of each one — the way they serve you or could be appreciated by the right person. Then look for evidence of actually being liked, not by people you've performed for, but by people who have seen you and chosen to be in your life anyway. Connection happens when you stop trying to be liked and start being yourself fully. The people who like the real you are the only ones whose liking actually feeds you — and they're already there if you look.
gentlePrompts: Think of someone whose face lights up when they see you.
Think of someone who reaches out to you without being prompted.

teaching: I am misunderstood often forms from dynamics where your inner experience wasn't received accurately — caregivers who interpreted your behavior wrongly, dismissed your feelings, or projected their own meanings onto you. It can also come from being a kid who saw the world differently than the people around you and got tired of explaining yourself. The wound shows up as frustration in conversations, a habit of over-explaining to make sure you're understood, retreating into silence when explaining feels too exhausting, and sometimes a deep loneliness that comes from feeling like nobody really gets you. People with this wound often abandon the work of being understood because it feels impossible — but the abandoning is part of what keeps the wound alive. Here's the key reprogramming insight from the PDS framework — understanding has to come from inside first. People in your life see you through their own filter of beliefs and programs, so they're never going to perfectly get you. But you can. The work is developing emotional literacy — labeling your feelings, knowing your needs, articulating your experience to yourself first. Then communicating that to others vulnerably, not because you're guaranteed they'll understand, but because the act of expressing honors the belief that your inner world is worth knowing. Over time, the people who can hear you find their way to you, and you become more skilled at recognizing when you've actually been understood.
gentlePrompts: Think of someone who finishes your thought correctly.
Think of a moment when someone said 'I get it' and they actually did.

teaching: I am disconnected has a specific quality compared to alone or excluded — it's the felt sense of going through the motions of relationships without the deep current of real contact. It often forms from one-to-one relationships in childhood where physical presence was there but emotional presence wasn't — a parent who was there but distracted, a sibling who was nearby but never really tuned in. As an adult, it shows up as loneliness in a room full of people, going through interactions on autopilot, and sometimes a habit of subconsciously sabotaging closeness when it gets too real because the unfamiliar intimacy feels unsafe. People with this wound often disconnect from themselves before disconnecting from others — numb to their own feelings, out of touch with their body, going days without checking in. That self-disconnection then gets projected onto relationships, creating the very disconnection you're afraid of. The reprogramming work is to reconnect with yourself first. Sit with your feelings. Notice your body. Journal what's actually happening inside. As you become more present to yourself, presence with others becomes possible — and you start to recognize the moments of real connection that have been there all along but you were too disconnected internally to feel them. Eye contact that lands. Conversations that leave you feeling more yourself. Those are real, and finding them retrains your nervous system to expect contact rather than distance.
gentlePrompts: Think of a conversation that left you feeling more yourself, not less.
Think of a moment of eye contact that landed.

teaching: I am excluded is different from not belonging — it's specific, situational, and triggered by particular moments of being left out. It often forms from group dynamics in childhood — being the kid not invited, feeling left out by siblings, watching friends do things without you. It can also come from family dynamics where you felt second-place to a sibling or a parent's attention. The wound has its own particular pain — a fear of missing out, hypersensitivity to invitations not received, and sometimes a baseline expectation that you'll be the one left out which makes you scan situations for evidence of exclusion. As an adult, this shows up as anxiety when group plans are made without your involvement, hurt feelings when friends do something without you even when it's reasonable, and sometimes pulling away preemptively to avoid the pain of being left out. The reprogramming work has a behavioral component that matters — practice asking for inclusion. People with this wound often suffer silently while assuming everyone else knows they want to be included. Saying 'hey, I'd love to be part of this' is a small act with disproportionate power. Then look for the evidence of inclusion you already have — invitations that came unprompted, groups that made room, people who reached out. The wound shifts as you take active responsibility for your inclusion rather than waiting for it to be given perfectly.
gentlePrompts: Think of an invitation that came without you having to ask.
Think of a group that made room when you arrived.

teaching: I am disrespected often forms from environments where you were talked down to, dismissed, or treated as if your opinions and feelings didn't carry weight. It can come from parents who didn't take you seriously as a child, siblings who mocked you, teachers who diminished you, or partners who consistently overrode your judgment. The wound shows up as hypersensitivity to perceived slights, anger when interrupted, defensiveness when questioned, and sometimes a habit of preemptively asserting yourself in ways that feel disproportionate to the moment. You might find yourself ruminating after meetings, replaying interactions to check whether someone was being respectful or condescending. Underneath the wound is often the suspicion that you don't actually deserve respect, which makes external disrespect doubly painful — it confirms what you secretly fear. The reprogramming work starts with respecting yourself. Are you treating your own opinions, time, and boundaries as worthy of respect? Often this wound is paired with self-disrespect that nobody can override. Then look for evidence of being respected in your existing relationships — moments where your judgment was deferred to, your work was taken seriously, your word held weight. Practice carrying yourself as someone who is respected. Over time the nervous system updates from scanning for disrespect to receiving the respect that's already there, and you become more discerning about which situations actually require your defensiveness.
gentlePrompts: Think of someone who deferred to your judgment on something that mattered.
Think of a moment when someone took your work or your word seriously.

teaching: I am rejected often pairs with disliked but has its own texture — it's specifically about being pushed away, turned down, or chosen against. It usually forms from significant rejections in childhood — a friendship that ended, a parent who emotionally rejected you, being chosen against in some moment that landed hard. Like the disliked wound, its roots are often in self-rejection — parts of yourself you've judged as unworthy and tried to hide, projecting that judgment outward. The wound shows up as social anxiety, fear of putting yourself out there, taking ambiguous situations as evidence of rejection, and sometimes a self-protective tendency to reject others first before they can reject you. People with this wound often pre-reject themselves on behalf of others — assuming someone won't be interested, deciding not to apply for the job, declining the invitation because the rejection feels inevitable. The reprogramming work starts with accepting the parts of yourself you've rejected. What have you tried to hide, minimize, or change about yourself? Make peace with those parts as belonging to your wholeness. Then look for evidence of acceptance — people who have seen your full self and stayed, yeses you received that you weren't sure were coming. Practice taking risks where rejection is possible, knowing that the pain of pre-rejecting yourself is greater than the pain of an external no. Acceptance starts inside, and the world starts mirroring it back as you do the work.
gentlePrompts: Think of someone who knows your full self and stays anyway.
Think of a yes you received that you weren't sure was coming.

teaching: I am trapped often forms from childhood circumstances where you felt controlled and had no agency — strict or authoritarian parents, family rules that didn't allow negotiation, an environment where you had needs but no power to get them met. It can also come from situations later in life where you felt stuck — relationships, jobs, or living situations where leaving felt impossible. The emotions that come with this wound are frustration, suffocation, resentment, and sometimes a kind of low-grade rage at the constraints around you. As an adult, this shows up as overvaluing freedom to the point of struggling with commitment, an allergy to feeling constrained, and sometimes the opposite — staying stuck in situations that don't serve you because you've internalized the helplessness. Some people overcompensate by trying to control everything in their lives to never feel trapped again, which ironically becomes its own kind of cage. The reprogramming work involves recognizing the freedom you already have. Across the seven areas of life, where do you have choice? Where do you exercise autonomy? Where do you have a say in your day, your time, your relationships? Most people with this wound underestimate the freedom they actually have because their nervous system is scanning for evidence of constraint. Then practice exercising freedom deliberately — making small choices that are fully yours, walking away from things that don't serve you, claiming your time. The wound shifts as you experience your own agency and your nervous system updates its expectations.
gentlePrompts: Think of a recent choice you made — even a small one — that was fully yours.
Think of a moment when you walked away from something that wasn't serving you.

aiPhrasing: times you have been moving forward
teaching: I am stuck is a close cousin of trapped but has a different quality — it's not just feeling constrained, it's the sense that nothing is changing no matter what you do. It often forms from childhood circumstances that felt static and out of your control — family dynamics that didn't change despite your attempts to help, situations where progress was impossible. As an adult, this wound shows up as a pervasive sense of treading water, frustration with your own perceived lack of progress, and sometimes a depressive flatness where action feels futile. People with this wound often discount actual progress because their nervous system is calibrated to a baseline of stuck — small forward motion doesn't register as significant. The reprogramming work involves recognizing all the ways you've been moving that you haven't been crediting. What's different about your life than a year ago? What habits have shifted? What relationships have evolved? What have you let go of? What have you become more skilled at? Most people with this wound have been moving substantially but haven't let the movement count emotionally. Then practice noticing small forward motion daily — a difficult conversation you had, a habit you maintained, a step you took on a project. Repetition of small movement, recognized and credited, retrains your nervous system to register progress rather than scanning for evidence of stuckness. Over time, the felt sense shifts from stagnation to flow.
gentlePrompts: Think of something in your life that's different than it was a year ago.
Think of a small step you took recently that took real effort.

teaching: I am powerless often forms from childhood situations where you genuinely couldn't change what was happening — abuse, neglect, family chaos, watching parents struggle with things outside your influence. It can also come from being in environments where your voice didn't carry weight, your no didn't matter, your influence over your own life was minimal. The emotions that come with this wound are despair, rage, defeat, and sometimes a chronic resignation that becomes a personality trait. As an adult, this shows up as not advocating for yourself, accepting circumstances you don't actually have to accept, and sometimes overcompensating by trying to control everything because powerlessness feels intolerable. People with this wound often have far more power than they're using, but their nervous system has been calibrated to expect powerlessness and they don't reach for the levers they actually have. The reprogramming work involves recognizing where you already have power — over your time, your choices, your responses, your boundaries, your relationships. Then practicing power deliberately — making decisions that are fully yours, holding lines that matter, speaking truth even when it's uncomfortable. The opposite of powerlessness isn't dominating others — it's the felt knowledge that you can shape what's possible in your own life. Each small act of agency, repeated with intention, rewires the nervous system from scanning for helplessness to expecting agency.
gentlePrompts: Think of a time you said no and held the line.
Think of a situation you shaped because of what you did.

teaching: I have no control is close to powerless but has its own specific texture — it's about the moment-to-moment sense of being at the mercy of circumstances, unable to direct your own life. It often forms from chaotic childhood environments where outcomes felt random, parents whose moods shaped your day without warning, or situations where the rules kept changing. The wound shows up as anxiety, hypervigilance about anticipating what might happen next, and sometimes a need to control everything as overcompensation. People with this wound often try to plan their way out of uncertainty, micromanage their environment, or alternately collapse into passivity because if you can't control anything, why try? Both are responses to the same underlying belief. The reprogramming insight is recognizing that you don't have to control what happens — you have agency over how you respond. That distinction matters enormously. You can't control whether someone reacts well to your boundary, but you can set the boundary. You can't control the outcome of the meeting, but you can prepare and show up well. Across the seven areas of life, look for where you exercise agency — your responses, your choices, your inputs, your effort. Then practice making deliberate decisions, however small, that remind your nervous system that you have hands on the wheel of your own life. The shift is from scanning for chaos to recognizing the agency that's already operating in you.
gentlePrompts: Think of a moment you chose your response, even when you couldn't choose what happened.
Think of a recent decision that was fully yours to make.

aiPhrasing: times you have been held in trust
teaching: I am betrayed often forms from significant breaches of trust in childhood — a parent who broke a promise that mattered, a friend who told your secret, a sibling who took your side and then turned. Sometimes it comes from a single specific event that landed hard, sometimes from a pattern of small betrayals that accumulated into expectation. The wound shows up as difficulty trusting, hypervigilance for signs of deception, holding people at arm's length even when they've earned closer access, and sometimes preemptive withdrawal when relationships start feeling significant. People with this wound often interpret neutral events through a betrayal lens — a delayed text, a forgotten plan, an absent moment — and feel pain disproportionate to the actual situation. Underneath is the protection of never being caught off guard again. The reprogramming work involves looking for evidence of being held in trust — people who have kept their word, chosen your interests even when inconvenient, shown up when they didn't have to. Then practicing trust deliberately in small ways, knowing that the cost of pre-betraying yourself by withholding trust is greater than the cost of occasional disappointment. Real intimacy requires the risk of being let down, and the wound shifts as you collect evidence that most of the people in your life have actually been trustworthy when given the chance to be.
gentlePrompts: Think of someone who kept their word to you when no one was watching.
Think of someone who chose your interest over their own convenience.
